title: Unifying human development and family science: Navigating identity challenges in higher education
abstract: Co-Guest editors Ramona Faith Oswald and Anisa Zvonkovic invited us to comment on the article by Dyer (2024) in the special issue, Theorizing Academia: Critical and Futuristic Perspectives. In her article, Dyer, as a faculty member at a highly regarded teaching-focused state university, offers her perspective on how Human Development and Family Science evolved from Home Economics. We agree with many of Dyer's thoughts, but our perspective deviates from the author's in some cases, perhaps due in part to our own scholarly and institutional experiences. There are multiple commonalities amongst the co-authors of this commentary that we wish to first highlight: We are all current administrators of academic units that offer doctoral degrees at research-intensive public universities. Beyond these characteristics, we bring with us distinct personal and academic backgrounds, varied department names, diverse placements within our universities' academic structures, and different university types (see Table 1 which briefly describes our training and current institutions to see this variability). Note that although our academic units may have slightly different names, such as Family Social Science or Human Development and Family Science(s), in this commentary we refer to the field as HDFS as this is the most common program name and the explicit subject of the target article. This common language highlights a shared disciplinary identity amongst departments, even when our specific names differ. Department Head and Professor of Human Development and Family Sciences at the University of Connecticut (UConn). Previously, faculty at the Pennsylvania State University, in the Department of Human Development and Family Studies, which is housed in the College of Health and Human Development We detail our own academic backgrounds and institutions to highlight one of the defining features of HDFS as a discipline – that our roots are interdisciplinary. We want to emphasize that while we have varied disciplinary training, and indeed most of us do not have degrees in HDFS, HDFS is our current academic home because of its interdisciplinary roots. Due to the expansive nature of HDFS, scholars from varied disciplines including Sociology, Developmental Psychology, Clinical Psychology, Social Psychology, Public Health, Social Work, and Education can teach and conduct research that explores human development in the context of families, interpersonal relationships, communities, and culture. Indeed, these fields have long contributed to the interdisciplinary nature of HDFS, along with other disciplines (Hollinger, 2002). We agree with the author that it is important to focus on HDFS holistically and expansively, rather than assuming a more singular view of HDFS, but also highlight that HDFS is a discipline in itself. We suggest further that a both/and orientation is needed to recognize that we are a single discipline that is built from an interdisciplinary framework, and such an approach must necessarily counter the urge to narrowly define and silo us. Counter to the author's perspective, however, we argue that we are not in an identity crisis. In fact, as scholars we have a consensus about the defining features of the HDFS field (Hollinger, 2002). We instead suggest that we know who we are but have a PR challenge in that we need to better communicate our identity to others and increase visibility (National Council on Family Relations, 2023). This challenge has clear and significant implications for administrators and our HDFS communities. A contributing factor to this PR challenge is that some social science disciplines are more narrowly focused or align with a more clearly defined career trajectory, so it is natural for individuals from these fields to attempt to apply the same narrow focus to HDFS. However, a strength of HDFS is that we train students for a variety of career pathways, and the interdisciplinary training HDFS offers is highly valuable to students who will enter a workforce in which job and career changes are increasingly common (Lyons et al., 2015). Moreover, from our discussions as administrators, most mission statements of HDFS departments across the United States very clearly and consistently articulate their training, research, and service. To summarize these mission statements: HDFS studies human development, relationships, and families across the lifespan, or put another way, HDFS studies human development in the context of families and other interpersonal relationships, communities, policies, and culture. Although these processes in relation to the culture and experiences of marginalized group members were not addressed until recent decades, our collective research and discovery efforts are solution-based and comprehensive. They are designed to address (a) physical, cognitive/mental, social, and emotional development across the lifespan, (b) family and relationship systems, (c) health disparities, (d) social justice, (e) community engagement, (f) applied work and interventions, and (g) translation of research to policy – all with the intent to improve the health and well-being of individuals, relationships, families, and communities. We see HDFS as having a core purpose to investigate and improve lives, but our approaches (within and across departments) to research questions necessarily differ. For example, some HDFS scholars do lab-based observation work, others do community-based participatory action research, others do randomized controlled trials, and still others focus on qualitative approaches, to name just a few methodologies (Dyer, 2022). We believe this breadth is rooted in our history of grappling with complex social topics and providing tools that help individuals and families thrive. We also recognize that tackling such topics requires a systems lens (whether focused on family systems or larger societal systems) that is multifaceted and multidisciplinary. Certainly, the diverse naming of our academic units contributes to this PR challenge, and as Dyer (2024) notes, there is often a mismatch between our disciplinary identity and the names of our academic units. Indeed, another challenge noted by Dyer is that some HDFS units have been combined with other academic units to create departments that obscure the HDFS identity (e.g., Counseling, Human Development, and Family Science; Education and Human Development). For other units, HDFS may be a program or major within a larger academic unit. The majority of HDFS faculty views this diversity and mismatch of academic unit names as problematic (Hans, 2014). With over 100 different names for HDFS academic units, outsiders often do not understand our discipline (Hans, 2014). Through organizational efforts of the National Council on Family Relations, a number of academic units have renamed their departments from Family Studies to Family Science/s or to Human Development and Family Science/s and helped to create a unified academic identity (National Council on Family Relations, 2023). However, NCFR has mostly focused on the Family Science side of our academic departments and has not pushed for a unified name for Human Development and Family Science. Although it is difficult to create an argument for uniform academic unit names as these names often differ for good reasons, such as unit composition or location within the university's academic structure (e.g., school/college), when it fits well, we encourage departments to consider updating their names to HDFS to provide a more consistent brand to our discipline. We also advocate for departments to make the “S” stand for science, rather than studies, to reflect that HDFS is a social science discipline, a movement in the field that has been in existence since the 1980s (National Council on Family Relations, 2023). This change would be one step in decreasing the PR challenge, recognizing the academic rigor of the field, and having national and global recognition of the discipline. Moreover, we argue that we need both Human Development and Family Science to adequately capture the breadth and depth of our disciplinary identity. Although the concerted efforts of the National Council on Family Relations and individual scholars to define and promote Family Science are admirable (e.g., Gavazzi et al., 2014; Hamon & Smith, 2014; National Council on Family Relations, 2022), by only naming one half of our identity in promotional efforts and academic discussions, we potentially weaken our brand identity and omit an important focus of our discipline. We agree with Dyer (2024) who argues, “the combination of developmental science and family science seems to provide a productive and stable academic department. These two disciplines may complement one another in ways that enhance both” (p. 12). We also agree with Dyer on the value of integrating Developmental Science and Family Science in training and research, including in her own work. However, we depart from Dyer's framing of this merger as mainly one of bureaucratic solidarity. HDFS has its origins in the field of Home Economics, which emerged in the late 19th and early 20th centuries (Dreilinger, 2021). Home Economics focused on improving family life, nutrition, child-rearing practices, and household management, often with a strong emphasis on women's roles. Although the study of Human Development and Family Science emerged later in Home Economics programs, for over 100 years, HDFS has been part of Home Economics (Dreilinger, 2021; Ermer & Roach, 2023). Our discipline has long combined both Developmental and Family Science principles. One cannot fully understand human development without a relational lens (including family relationships), just as one cannot fully understand the family system without a developmental lens. Further, we argue that our HDFS identity is a gestalt, and thus our field is bigger and more impactful through this combination. By promoting HDFS as a discipline bigger than the sum of its parts, we acknowledge our multidisciplinary roots but are also able to move forward as one discipline that integrates the depth and breadth of these roots. The U.S. Department of Education (DOE) collects and reports data about academic programs on campuses and categorizes the academic programs into a developed classification of instructional program, commonly referred to as CIP codes (National Center for Education Statistics, n.d.). These CIP codes are used to map programs to a shared understanding of their content, collect data from schools on programs of study offered, and create reports on educational trends for government and public use. They are intended to represent the curricular aspects of a major or degree field. There are three levels of CIP codes: 2-digit, 4-digit, and 6-digit. The 2-digit prefix represents the broadest scope of the discipline, and the 6-digit code represents the most specific (National Center for Education Statistics, n.d.). Each higher education institution reports data, based on the CIP, to the National Center for Education Statistics (NCES). CIP codes, as noted by Dyer et al. (2024), are generally invisible to faculty and students and are typically assigned by administrators, sometimes beyond department heads, who may not understand the nuances of a particular field. CIP codes have important implications not only for academic programs and institutions but also for individual students. From an institutional perspective, CIP codes standardize reporting for enrollments and degrees, enable program comparisons across institutions, assess program breadth and depth, inform accountability and performance metrics, and determine the applicability of federal regulations to programs. From a student's perspective CIP codes impact eligibility for some state and federal financial aid programs, student loan forgiveness programs, and may impact visa eligibility and extensions for certain international students. From a faculty perspective, CIP codes are sometimes used to determine eligibility for special programs or grant funding. In addition, funding and resource allocation may vary by institution, state, or federal funding. CIP codes are also examined in reference to graduation rates or job placement and are often linked to CIP codes for accountability reporting to state and federal agencies, ultimately potentially impacting enrollment in programs with high demand CIP codes.
